title: PSS3 Q2 — Search a Row/Col-Sorted Matrix
statement: An $n \times n$ matrix where each row is strictly increasing left-to-right and each column is strictly increasing top-to-bottom. Test whether a given integer $b$ is in the matrix. Design: $O(n^2)$, then $O(n \log n)$ via binary search per row, then a D&C, then $O(n)$.

steps.title: $O(n^2)$ — brute force.
steps.body: Check each of the $n^2$ entries. Done.

steps.title: $O(n \log n)$ — binary search per row.
steps.body: Each row is sorted: binary-search each row for $b$ in $O(\log n)$. Total $O(n \log n)$.

steps.title: $O(n \log n)$ via D&C (split into 4 quadrants).
steps.body:
Split the matrix into four $n/2 \times n/2$ quadrants. Compare $b$ to the center element. Based on comparisons, some quadrants are ruled out (but not always — in general 3 of the 4 can remain).

Recurrence: $T(n) = 3T(n/2) + O(1)$, giving $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 3}) \approx \Theta(n^{1.58})$. **Worse than $O(n \log n)$.**

tips.label: Why 3 quadrants, not 2
tips.content: Comparing to the center rules out only one quadrant (the one whose maximum is $< b$ or whose minimum is $> b$). The other three must still be searched. Hence $3T(n/2)$.

steps.title: $O(n)$ — walk from a corner.
steps.body:
Start at the top-right corner $(1, n)$. At each step, compare the current cell to $b$:

- If equal, return found.
- If cell $> b$: $b$ can't be in this column (everything below is even larger). Move left.
- If cell $< b$: $b$ can't be in this row (everything to the left is even smaller). Move down.

Stop when you fall off the matrix.

**Why $O(n)$.** Each step moves either left or down by one. You can do at most $n$ left-moves and $n$ down-moves before falling off. So $O(n)$ steps total.

**Correctness.** When you move left from $(i, j)$ because $M[i,j] > b$, every entry in column $j$ at row $\ge i$ is $\ge M[i,j] > b$. So those are eliminated. Similarly for down-moves.

tips.label: Can we do better than $O(n)$?
tips.content: No — there's a lower bound of $\Omega(n)$ based on an adversary argument. In the worst case, you essentially must traverse a staircase of $n$ cells.

answerKey: Best: $O(n)$ via staircase walk from top-right. Binary search per row gives $O(n \log n)$; D&C into quadrants is $\Theta(n^{\log_2 3})$ — worse.
